title: Peripheral Devices and I/O Methods
summary: Describes peripheral device structure, the I/O controller, memory-mapped vs. isolated I/O, and the three communication modes: programmed I/O, DMA, and interrupt-driven I/O.
content_points: Peripheral = device + I/O controller (with data, status, and command registers).
Memory-mapped I/O: peripheral registers mapped into main memory address space; same read/write signals.
Isolated I/O: separate address space and control signals for peripherals.
Programmed I/O (polling): CPU loops waiting for device – wastes cycles.
DMA: controller transfers data directly between device and memory; CPU bypassed; efficient for bulk transfers.
Interrupt-driven I/O: device notifies CPU via interrupt when ready; CPU does other work meanwhile.
learning_objectives: Describe the I/O controller register model
Compare programmed I/O, DMA, and interrupt-driven I/O
